event: The Miracle of the Child Attacked and Rescued by Blessed Augustine Novello
symbols: falling child — human vulnerability and the need for divine intervention
Augustine Novello appearing at the scene — saintly intercession and the communion of saints
fortified tower and city — the earthly city with its dangers, contrasted with divine protection
gold background — the heavenly realm breaking into the temporal world through miraculous intervention
kneeling mother — prayerful supplication and faith in divine mercy
description: A Gothic panel painting with a gold background depicting a dramatic scene set against a medieval fortified city with a tall crenellated tower. In the upper portion, a child falls or has fallen from a high balcony of the stone building. Below, a figure in dark Augustinian robes -- identified as Augustine Novello -- bends over the injured child lying on the ground, performing the miraculous rescue. A woman in a red garment rushes toward the scene with arms outstretched in anguish. Several other figures, including onlookers and witnesses, observe from doorways and the street.
theological_description: This panel from Simone Martini's altarpiece for Blessed Augustine Novello (Agostino Novello, d. 1309) depicts one of the posthumous miracles attributed to this Augustinian friar and papal legate. The scene belongs to the broader tradition of hagiographic miracle panels that demonstrate the communion of saints -- the belief that the holy dead continue to intercede for the living (Hebrews 12:1). The rescue of the child from certain death through saintly intervention reflects the scriptural principle that God delivers the vulnerable and hears the cry of the afflicted (Psalm 34:17-18). The miraculous healing evokes the raising of the Shunammite's son by Elisha (2 Kings 4:32-37) and the raising of Eutychus by Paul after falling from a window (Acts 20:9-12). The gold background signifies the interpenetration of the heavenly and earthly realms, the moment when eternity breaks into time through divine compassion. The mother's anguished rush toward her child echoes the grief of the widow of Nain (Luke 7:11-15), whose son was restored to her by Christ. These miracle panels served a catechetical function, teaching that the God who raised the dead through prophets and apostles continues to work through His saints.

scripture_refs.reason: Eutychus falling from a window and being raised by Paul directly parallels the child falling from a balcony and being rescued through saintly intercession.
Elisha raising the Shunammite's son from death prefigures the miraculous rescue of a child through a holy man's intercession.
Christ restoring the widow of Nain's son parallels the mother's child being returned to her through divine mercy.
The Lord hears the cry of the righteous and delivers them from all their troubles.
The great cloud of witnesses -- the communion of saints whose intercession is visually represented by Augustine Novello's miraculous appearance.
The tradition of holy men raising the dead, connecting Old Testament prophetic miracles to the ongoing economy of saintly intercession.

_meta.title: The Miracle of the Child Attacked and Rescued by Augustine Novello
